speaker: interviewer
text: We are interviewing Mr. Yuhudah Bakon. It's tape number 12. And now you've--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. It's tape number 14. And you promised to tell us about how you started your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, I married the very first time and I was just over 30.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married twice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I had a girl in the first marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got married in which year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: '61. Something around '60, '61. And then I had a little girl. After a couple of years, we divorced. And soon afterwards, I married again. And from this marriage, I have two boys, who are now 24 and 25.

speaker: interviewer
text: So actually, you had more influence on the education of your boys than of your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's true. But even my boys didn't have it so easily. Because they could breathe in the influence of a father who was in the camp. And apart from it, they were very-- suffered very much in their childhood. And even later, with all kinds of illnesses I wouldn't like to go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which were sort of hereditary from you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. But I wasn't a great help. And they suffer very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, in what way you weren't great help?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was busy with my teaching and with my art. And my wife had to sacrifice her career. And later on, I had to sacrifice many years too, until today, just to help our children. And if you don't have any relatives, my sister was living in [INAUDIBLE]. And she was far away. It was very, very tough. And is still very tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife, also, doesn't have any family here in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Only mother and sister died. So both of us didn't have anybody who would help us--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who would help you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: --bring up the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. But tell me, did the education you gave your children, did it have any-- did it affect them, the fact that you were in the camps? I mean, the stories, did you tell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, surely they know much more than other children. Just a little example, when children go for holiday, and everyone says, oh, I go to visit my uncle. Oh, I go to visit my grandparents, my children had nobody. And I had to adopt as if one of my elderly students, and they called her grandma. And she could give them a sweetie and so on. And they could go to her when they were small. It's a situation that somebody who is not a former-- I mean, somebody without parents, orphaned, and is now more or less in the same situation, can't imagine. And their whole life was, for me and for them, like that. I mean, even in Israel, if you didn't have very good friends or parents or uncles-- and I had nobody in Jerusalem-- it was very, very tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only imaginable for other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your sons made it. I mean, both of them are--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no they didn't made it yet. I hope they will made it. They're studying. But they are very, very physically sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Both of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I will not go into details.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, we shall not, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: We fight for their life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I met your son and he's such a fine-looking young guy.

words.text: Because I met your son and he's such a

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I don't want to go in details.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. We shall not. And we shall also not speak about how much the camps affected your painting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that you can--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because we shall speak about it when we look at your paintings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we shall see. And then you will explain that-- all these things. Well, unless there is something very important that we didn't speak about that you would like to say--

speaker: interviewee
text: In which connection?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything. If there is something that you feel that you would like to say, because we are going soon, to finish this interview. So I want to ask you a last question. But before I ask the last question, maybe you want to say something? You would like to say something before I ask you the last question?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only would mention that after the war I wanted to dedicate my life to art. And in the beginning, especially, to show what happened to a child, and the great disappointment after the war, that nobody cared and nobody was better or changed for better. And about this, I thought quite a lot. And the main problem was the meaning of suffering and the meaning, what happened to us, if it has a meaning and so on. And this was a question which were very, very important for me. And I wanted to somehow relate it with others of us who came through our experience. And then I wanted to say, how art helped me to overcome many problems. Because I know other of my friends didn't have art as a healing power. Maybe the other helps, good friends or family and so on. So I'm very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: --thankful to these wonderful people who helped me to survive after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also to art, you are thankful.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the art, yeah. And then I am happy that I could give something from my experience, the positive things, through the education. Because I was an art teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Are you still teaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. and now I want, really, with the last strength I still have and hope to have a little longer, to help-- to do what I couldn't do through all kinds of circumstances. I hope I will still be able to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'd like-- before we close this interview, I'd like to ask you, after all you have been through-- and this is really a very special story-- is there a special message that you have for the future, for the coming generations, who will maybe not know much about the Holocaust, as we do know? And maybe you would like to say some words about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not only some words. I think that one of the main things, if you really deeply think about it, if there is a meaning in all this suffering, if suffering has a meaning or could have a meaning, I say, yes. I don't think people should go through suffering. But if the whole thing is how you can change your life experience towards something good. Meaning that you can help other people for better understanding. You deepen your understanding. If you are in the abyss of death, and that we actually had for a very long time, and you overcome it, and you actually go in a different direction, usually you go from youth to death. We went from death to life after the war. And I always said, well, we are children of 16. But somehow, we are 80 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning we had the experience of 80 years. How can we bring it together? That was a very strange situation. Because when I saw other children, or youngsters in my age, I was envy that they had a normal life or they have a normal life, that they have parents that they can speak with in a normal way. But on the other hand, I knew they have-- they are lacking something which they will not have. And that is the experience of these terrible years. And what can you do with this experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I believe if you go very, very deep in, you can have better knowledge of the other human being. And this is one of the main theme in my life, that through suffering some doors are opened quicker or better or meaningful-er towards other human being. I also learned something else. That this abyss of evil is nearly in all human beings. I don't see that in everyone. Usually through our education and so on, it's covered. But it's a very thinly covered. I experience that most educated people changed into wild animals. And I experience that most simple people changed into saints.

speaker: interviewer
text: So again, if you are at the abyss or in the abyss or just before death, what is left? What is left? And we were in this situation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing was left. We know that they annihilate all of us. Nobody is left. There is not like, in a normal family, OK, I die. But I have an uncle. Somebody will remember. I knew-- I mean, at that time we thought it like that-- nothing, nobody will be left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you behave in such a situation? What is the most important thing? And of course, this idea went deeper and deeper. And you ask about meaning of life. And everyone came to this conclusion. I couldn't bear that somebody said, well, I was saved. And here you come to believe, God saved me. But what is with the other one, whom God didn't save? And here you come, really, to the very deepest of philosophical religious question.

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't accept that somebody said, God was good to me. But he wasn't good to others.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my question is what kind of God is it, that you believe in him only when you are OK? But in the minute somebody dies or you have it terribly, you lose your belief? It's not a belief. It's kind of adultery. And how you call it? An Avodah Zarah. You believe in something which is only good as long as you have it good. And then you think about what is really good and what is really something else. Evil.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was tremendously lucky that I had wonderful teachers. Not only teachers, but human beings. Maybe in my opinion, the best which at that time, the Jews produced. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if you can say. Leo Baeck, who I knew from Theresienstadt. But here, Hugo Bergmann and Martin Buber. I still remember after the war, he gave a lecture. He said, many people write to me, how can you still believe in God? How can you say, [HEBREW] after what happened? That was immediate after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you believe and change into a different one, in a much deeper and meaningful one. Not such an easy answer, I survived. That was one of the main themes which I tried to struggle with.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, also, what can you do afterwards? You can give further on what you learned through your experience. And I believe the best thing you can do is to deepen this knowledge. And well, this world is today, a little-- it has not now, the same evaluation. But they evaluated this word. But that is love, better understanding. And that was the greatest healing power I experienced. Or I could cry if somebody was good to me after the war. If somebody really met me and tell-- and told me a word. If he was-- you know, today people speak and don't mean anything. But children are very sensitive. Who speaks with them or who just speaks above them or use them as an object, but not as another human being. And I tried to give this feeling to my student. Of course, it's important that I do teach them how to draw, et cetera, et cetera. But to give him a feeling that you care, that you are with them, that you understand them. And I'm happy to say, that many years afterwards, I met students who remembers me and who wrote me, so on. And this, you remember from a teacher. And of course, we don't succeed always. But we try.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much. On behalf of the survivors of the Shoah Foundation, really, it was an experience for us to interview you. Thank you, Mr. Bakon.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. You want names or so, or not?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandfather and grandmother from father's side. And my grandfather died still normally, and has a gravestone in Bruenn. My grandmother died in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was about '39, '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yohanan ?] and [? Scheindel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the family name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bakon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bakon. We don't want that head.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, Y'Israel and [? Etel ?], or Esther Bakon. Most probably, when they married. My parents and my sisters. And I most probably am here, one year or so, a very small child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So which year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. But it should be '31 or so. That's family Bakon, mainly. The upper row is an aunt, her husband, my mother, with the tie. My sister, [NON-ENGLISH], my aunt Clara, my uncle [? Baruch. ?] Then the men standing up is [INAUDIBLE] Bakon, who was the dean of Yeshiva University in New York. He still lives there. He's over 80. And his sisters and his brother on the corner, [? Shimon, ?] who lives now in Israel. And the first row, my late cousin, Moshe. His sister, Miriam, who is still alive. I'm the smallest child, on the lap of my father. Sitting, my late sister, [? Hani ?] or Hannah. Standing over her is [NON-ENGLISH], who is living in Israel. My grandmother, [? Scheindel. ?] My grandfather, [? Yohanan. ?] My uncle, [? Herschel. ?] That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: On which occasion was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't know. I was too small. But surely in a summer holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Summer holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my family. It must be after, I think, September, October '41. Because then was the decree that we had to go with the Judenstern, with [NON-ENGLISH], with my sister, Hannah, on the left. And I am above my parents. I think it was near our deportation, the beginning, in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. It's my sister an me. And was probably also before the deportation in '42, from [NON-ENGLISH]. She has a [NON-ENGLISH], the Jewish star. I don't have it because I remember mine was sewn on the jacket. And I took it off. So on my shirt you can't see it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you enlarge it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is one of the last photos from our class before deportation. And in the middle you have the teacher, Leopold [? Levinger, ?] who was the headmaster for our class, about whom I talked. And when I saw him, how he was killed by a tractor in Theresienstadt. And you see for this festival kind of picture, we had to take white shirts. And of course, I'm the only one who didn't brought to school a white shirt. And I'm sitting the second or the third from the left, with a dark shirt, and pigeon kind of movement. This photograph was used in the Eichmann trial when they asked me-- Houser--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hausner.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hausner. How many children survived? And unfortunately, only two or three survived. And here is also, this story I told you, that I asked my friend, who is here, that we should meet a couple of weeks after the war. And of course, I was dreaming years afterwards that we came to this spot. And he didn't arrive. That is a Bible from the ghetto central [NON-ENGLISH], from Theresienstadt, where my teacher, Leopold [? Levinger, ?] who was killed in Theresienstadt, was a librarian. My sister wrote and gave me this Bible for my bar mitzvah, though she didn't know at all if I've alive. And hand it to me over in '46, when I arrived in Israel. Over 40, nearly 50 years, I tried to get hold of the music which was in my ear, the 'Dona Nobis Pacem', which we had to sing in the children's block. And recently, one singer got for me, the notes, the musical notes, 'Dona Nobis Pacem'. The transport list of my arrival from Theresienstadt to Auschwitz. And the name of the family when we arrived, and also the numbers. My mothers and sisters arrived into [NON-ENGLISH] Stutthof. One of Mengele's twins, who was looking for her identity for 45 years, and only recently we found documents in the Museum of Auschwitz, who she actually is. It's Eva [? Traup, ?] And here we can see Mengele's handwriting, asking for blood tests of Eva [? Traup ?] with her tattooed number. And therefore, we could establish her identity after so many years. One of the first painting of a Muselmann, after the war, in '45, when I was in the [INAUDIBLE]. One of my first drawing, from '45. I wanted to express a child escaping from the burning of Europe and so on. And on his shoulders, he carries the Torah, and leaving this country to another country. Meaning to Palestine, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you paint it?

speaker: interviewee
text: '45. It's signed. Just before transportation to Theresienstadt, the last piece of bread and cup of coffee, all the belongings are ready to take with, with the transport number. Mine was BL214.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it painted?

speaker: interviewee
text: At once, after the war. A kind of symbol of the Shoah, a Jewish child and the star. Some years after the war. I tried to express sorrow and suffering of the women. Also, symbol of death. In the background you have the crematorias of Auschwitz and this. The crematorium 1, 2, and 3, 4. I went with the two types. The one with one chimney, the other one with two chimneys. And above them, on the right side, you see a woman who is stamped. Sometimes the crematorium and the gas chambers were filled up. And so the people were stamped and had to wait till there is a place for them to go to the gas chambers. A Muselmann. Oh, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: A Muselmann. And it's signed with my tattooed number. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Here you see Auschwitz-Birkenau, carrying dead people. In the middle you have the barracks. And in the far background, you have the crematorias and also the gallows. One of my first drawing, where I wanted to show a colony of people going into the gas chambers, into the crematorium. The black sun, as if the end of the life and so on. And the SS men, with their heads accompanying them. This is the so-called [GERMAN], the chamber before the gas chamber, where people had to take off their clothes. You see the benches over them are they call out with numbers they had to remember, as I had told. And further on are the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, did you visit this place? Were you actually inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Otherwise how could I draw it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You saw it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is exactly what I saw. So looked the gas chamber when you had to look into it. You see the kind of shift. From above was thrown the Zyklon B. And on the sides, above was this installation which looked like a shower. And next to it were some electric lamp covered with very strong iron wire that they couldn't break it. And underneath were some ventilation equipment. This is suicide in the barbed wire-- electric barbed wire. And it's a woof engraving. The crematorium actually was on the side. But for the artistic effect, I put it in the middle. Otherwise, it's very realistic. And from the chimney, I put as if the face of the death of my father. Martin Buber and Leo Baeck who still signed, many years ago, the portraits I made of them. That is [INAUDIBLE], who is one of the righteous of the Gentiles, who has a tree in Yad Vashem and had a influence on all children who met him. Also, on grown ups. An extraordinary man. To commemorate his 100th birthday, UNESCO and the Czech Republic just gave us this medal of [INAUDIBLE]. A drawing where I wanted to show so I looked after the war. And it was also used for the Eichmann trial in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Self portrait?

speaker: interviewee
text: A kind of self portrait. My portrait, about the end of '45, beginning '46.
